title: Sheba A -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor
meeting_title: Sheba A -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor

transcript.text: Hi, Sheba.
How are you?
I'm very good, thanks, Andi.
Can you hear me okay?
Yes.
Can you hear me?
Yes, I can.
Great.
Where are you located, Sheba?
I'm in New York, so Eastern time.
Awesome.
Awesome.
I'm normally in New York, but this week I'm in, or I'm normally on Eastern time.
I'm usually in Rhode Island, but this week I'm in San Francisco.
Nice.
Yes.
Cool.
Well, thank you so much for scheduling some time to speak with us.
I can give you a little bit of a background on myself, GrowthX, and this role.
Then we can talk about you, and I'll try and leave 10-ish minutes at the end for you to ask questions, if that works.
Awesome.
So just very quickly, I have my headphones on.
I hope that's not an issue.
It's just really good for me to encele your sound, if that's okay.
Yeah, that's great.
Your video is a little...
A bit grainy, but, well, if that becomes a problem, we can just turn it off, but for now, let's try and keep it.
Okay.
Okay, cool.
So, I lead customer operations at GrowthX, and that means that I'm helping shape the processes, making sure that we have the right tech people and all the other aspects in place to deliver customers.
Our content to our clients.
GrowthX is really a services agency at its core.
We do have our own internal product that we have built and launched in June, which is what we're leveraging on the back end to generate all of our client content.
It is a newer product, but what it's basically doing is building workflows that use AI at different aspects and also build in...
Human in the loop sort of checkpoints to do the deep research, write the article outlines, generate content and all the things in between to get our clients, you know, content at scale.
And at scale is really a key word here because we're publishing a lot more than a traditional marketing agency might.
So each client, we're like getting out at a minimum five pieces a week and, you know, in various, like across industries, across various topics.
And so what that means for this role, we're supporting clients in pod format.
And this role is kind of a core project manager that wears two hats.
And this is sort of the way I'm thinking about evaluation criteria.
One piece is technical in terms of this is a new platform that we're leveraging and we need somebody who is able to communicate.

What's working and not working, how to make it better, and sort of can make that translation for the tech team, understands AI deeply enough to talk about the prompts and how we might or might not be accounting for the prompts in our workflows.
And then the other is like a project manager with a client facing bend to it.

So making sure that we're meeting the deliverables, the client knows where we are in the process, we're thinking about next steps, and also doing some of like the editing before anything gets published.

So you're the core, the technical managing editor is the core, we have a content manager layer that actually hits play on those workflows, generates first drafts, and then the managing editor like edits those.
And then we have an account strategist or account director, who is
Doing exactly what it sounds like, leading account strategy, thinking kind of a month or two ahead, helping us identify where we go next in terms of content generation and strategy with a client.

So they're building the roadmap, you're executing and making sure we're kind of hitting all those milestones, and then the content manager is generating that baseline content and the layer before it goes out.
Does that make sense?
Yeah, that makes sense.
That makes sense.
And then maybe I might turn my camera off, maybe to save your screen capacity.
be me.
My apologies.
I'm not sure what's going on.
Okay.
Yeah.
I mean, I think it's actually me.
Okay.
Well, why don't you start by, why don't you tell me a little bit about yourself?
Sure.
So, um, so I.
I would describe myself as a content strategist who works basically in the intersection between technical communication or technical software application and editorial.
How I arrived at this sort of hybrid place in my career was through, I had worked as a content strategist with Series A startups.
One is called Story Hunter, they're called Glimmer now, and the second was called Stringer.
So I began my career, actually the first company was called Brand Yourself.
So if you know anything about these companies, Brand Yourself was a Shark Tank alum, and they basically offered a reputation management software for clients who wanted to clean up their online image.

So I started off there as an account strategist, basically, and I worked on a slew of client accounts producing content for their social or editorial channels, which was sort of rank on Google, like search.
So that way we could kind of brand our clients.
So
Actually, after Brand Yourself, I ended up, I got into a grad program called Studio 20 at NYU, the journalism school.
It's a very unique program in that we weren't doing traditional reporting.
It was more of the intersection between editorial and human-computer interaction.

So as part of that program, we were placed in media companies as project management design people who would work on cross-functional projects that would kind of like align end-to-end design or content strategy projects.
Andrea, can you hear me?
Yes.
Hello?
Okay.
Sorry, just wanted to make sure you're there.
Yeah, yeah, yeah, I'm still here.
Great.
So from working at, sorry, after my grad program, I did some projects at the Wall Street Journal where I was basically a project manager slash content strategist.
I worked on some newsletter products they had.
They were trying to figure out which ones to sunset, which ones they needed to optimize.
I also did some work at Human Rights Rights Watch.
My final thesis.
And then I ended up getting into a project at Meta.
At that time, that team was called the Facebook journalism project.
So I did end to end project management for news literacy products and content research.
Because of that work I did at Meta, I ended up getting a role internally with their content strategy team on social impact products.
So I would say, and I can kind of give you up onto the last role I did, which I think is the most key, which I think makes me a great candidate for this role.
In my last role, I worked as a sole, basically like a content engineer for a company called Launch AI.
It's also a service-based SaaS product.
So essentially, Launch AI serves a real estate client base.
And that real estate client base, people come who are agents, homeowners.
Vendors who fix homes use the platform to send SMS messages or to produce content for their own businesses.

So typically my day, the workflow for me as the sole content engineer was sitting in a client meeting, figuring out like what their content needs were, what kind of content research had been done, learning from sales and the CX team, how we can build more content for that client.
Typically people would want a content library at the end or an SMS library at the end.

And I managed a team of QA writers who, after using AI to sort of scale contents for our client, we would then bring the AI writers in to do some quality assurance review and then hand over to the clients based on their asks.
So I can speak a little more in detail about that role because I think it's very key to this job, this position, which I'm really excited about.
So whatever you need me to kind of.
I go deeper on it.
I can.
I just wanted to give you a very quick level.
Thank you so much for that overview.
I really love your background.
It's so interesting.
Yeah, let's talk a little bit about your experience with the AI writers.
I guess we'll start with what did you learn that surprised you about AI content generation?

Well, I think what surprised me the most, I think, was first the ways in which we could design our metrics around the AI output and being able to also manage, in my case, client expectations and the expectations of C-suite based on that output.
So I was also brought into that role to basically build the content generator for the company.
And it required a lot of, like, long-form weeks of testing.
We created our own.

We, I mean, me and the Power BI team, I led the creation of prompt libraries that we could kind of use over and over, and it had to sort of be templatized, and then we would plug in the client's information.

So there's this back end of Launch AI software, which was before the content engineer engine idea, I would have to go into the back end on each client's account and create their messaging, and then they would see it in their account.
So as the company expanded and the business grew, they needed to figure out how we could turn three days of content creation to eight hours using AI, because we were getting a slew of clients.
that meant being able to create templatized systems, templatized workflows.
We use a lot of ClickUp in the organization, so that was also really helpful in terms of like the workflow and process.
As far as what I learned about AI, it's a bit difficult sometimes to manage client and internal expectations.
And one of the things that's important is...
Understanding your clients' metrics and being able to match that with the metrics that you're proposing, which is why people are coming to you, coming to your business in the first place.
Most clients have their own internal structure.
So how do you keep those two things in congruence and actually reflect an increase optimization scale?
In our case for SMS products, for SMS messaging, it was the MPS score.
So how can we improve the MPS score if we now have 10 clients as opposed to five?

I can speak a little more deeper into that, which obviously when you're working with a cross-functional team, there has to be like sources of truth, being able to implement best practices and sticking to the guns of the client's objective and the audience needs of the client.
So, yeah, totally.
I'm curious when you're, when you talk about, you know, aligning reporting as an outcome.
With, like, client expectations, how, I'm sure that you didn't have, like, a custom report for every client.
So, you know, what did you align on and how did you kind of help shape those metrics for the client?
Sure.
As I mentioned, I would specifically sit in on the sales and CX calls.
So, from those calls, which would be, like, the need finding or the discovery stage or content research stage, we would understand what the client's metrics were.
And, obviously, we're coming to these calls with, depending on the client's package that they have purchased with us, here's how we can improve these metrics for you.
So, in the world of conversation or dialogue or SMS, most people want to improve their open rate, their click-through rate, or their NPS score.
So, that would be a standard version.
But, sometimes people came to us for additional things, like, how do we...
How our campaign management?
How do we bring more buyers into our digital store?

So those are metrics that say keeping a standardized metric and then add-ons based on whatever else is being upsold, you know, in a business to the client or what can also create a client or customer retention to keep that person as a part of your business.
Just, you know, I'm taking notes, so just give me a second.
Sure.
Can I just stop?
Another addition.
So basically, like, my role was dual pronged.
It was the building of that content engine as well as manage the end-to-end workflow.
I was also basically writing the messages.
of twas.
exploiting teach So
So people were coming to us for personalized real estate messaging, which was either client acquisition, campaign management, or to deepen the engagement.
So after this content generator was created, I actually have a slide deck I can share.

Basically, I learned that the engineering team, they needed a template that if you've worked a lot in GPT, or if you know anything about template or structured data, the best way to structure your data is using JSON files.
You know, if you're, let's say your, your database is primarily like, uh, Python or SUL shaped, um, as far as the languages to create those data structures.
So I designed a JSON template for all the prompt into GPT.
Um, Um,
Are you there?
Sheba?
Yeah, I'm there.
I'm here.
sorry.
I'm not sure what happened.
Yeah, I lost you for a little bit, but honestly, I feel like I have run out of time.
No, no, no, no, no.
I feel like I don't need to ask you any more questions because I'm so impressed and you've pre-answered so many of my questions.
So, yeah, I don't want to lose you before we get to that point, but I want to see if you have any questions for me because I feel like you pre-answered so many of mine.
Okay.
I would like to make an apology.
I'm really excited about the existence of this role.
So my question to you is, what is, how are metrics basically designed or decided if there's a slew of clients and they maybe have different kinds of content with different needs?
Yeah, so in the scope of work, we outline like the work streams and the number of deliverables that we're going to like deliver for each client.
And quite honestly, that's where we're at today.
Like, are we meeting our contractual obligations and publishing or putting out like what we said we would for the client in there, like, you know, within the schedule that we said we would.
I think one of my goals for this quarter is to put in place like more CSAT metrics.
And in terms of like client metrics, right now we're using Looker dashboards and just like connecting to their GSC or GA for instance.
Nice.
To do like clicks and impressions.
That I think can and should get more sophisticated as we go, but isn't a main focus right now.
Not right now, it's just like deliverable.
And, like, content's performing, right?
So, yeah.
The reason why I ask is, because I've worked on a slew of products, right?
I've done the web work.
I have a background also in newsletters and social channels.
So I'm very curious at, like, what clients specifically come for.
Do they come for social?
Or is it web?
Or is it in-app?
Yeah, so it's web.
We're doing a lot of, like, I think our core, what we're best at right now is editorial blog posts.
Nice.
And, like, we do, like, an SEO audit.
But a lot of clients are really interested in geo and how they can, like, optimize for AI answers.
Nice.
Which we're leaning a lot on refreshes in terms of that work stream.
So, you know, if we have clients that have, like,,000-plus pages, and it's like, well, how old?
What's the formatting?
How do we kind of restructure them and refresh them so that like AI will notice them and they show up in results?
So those are our two core.
We're getting a lot more requests for thought leadership stuff and are building like we have some first some draft pipelines that we're using for a couple of our clients on thought leadership.

Not certainly not like the core of what we're doing, because I think like the, you know, making sure that you're like, have the tone and the persona of thought leader correct is just like a little bit of extra work.
And right now, you know, we want to try and keep our work streams relatively focused.
And then what else?
Yeah, like, we, as part of, we also do like programmatic.
So if like programmatic.
We have a template with a client and have kind of like agreed upon, Hey, like we can do a hundred, like we have a client that does like psychiatric services, like medical for like across the country.
Right.
So it's like, you know, depression therapy in X state.
And the content is pretty much the same, but you need 50 of those articles.
Or if you're doing it by city, like we just did 300 articles in a week.
So that's more about like building out the template, making sure that like we have all the right information and we're pulling in, you know, whatever blocks we need for each zone.
And then the pipeline can publish those programmatically.
That is also newer, but something that like we're getting better at doing with our own internal platform.
And I think it's really, we're seeing good.
Yeah.
Great.
I like that.
like, obviously, AI is growing.
So there's so many ways that we're applying it.
And I think that that's also defining what we don't know yet about metrics and new surfaces as well as it integrates with AI.
Yeah.
Great.
And my final question for you is, from your leadership perspective, what would you define as a successful technical manager, technical managing editor, excuse me?
Sorry, I was muted.
Because our platform is so new, I really want someone who knows, has the, like, not only editorial skill, but also, like, technical language.

To be able to communicate where they find that a workflow is breaking or going wrong, because we can generate articles at scale if the tech and the platform are working, but in order to, when something's not working, rather than say, it's broken, I'll just do it on my own by hand, that won't scale as we start to sign more and more deals.
So we need somebody that's willing to file the linear ticket and explain why they want it to do and why it's not doing that and where they think it's not working.
So editorial skills are hugely important, but that communication loop is going to be what gets us to the next level.
Yeah.
Yeah, I love, thank you for answering my questions.
It was also just like a pleasure to even know that this company existed.
I I think I've been
I've been trained for this.
When I went through my grad program, I think that the program director anticipated that there would be roles like this.
So I feel like everything I've been doing kind of leads up to this.
And I'm really excited and curious even about the work that's happening in the company.
Yeah.
I mean, it does seem like you have been trained for this in like three different ways.
So I, I'm definitely gonna like move you along and hopefully we, you know, get to move you to a panel interview and you can talk through kind of your process there too.
Sure.
Yeah.
Okay.
Sheba, is there anything else I can answer for you?
I guess I was, my final was just more, I understand a bit about the organizational structure.
Um, I think the one thing I would love to know is how are technical managing editors audited even?
Because I know there's the pod structure, right?
So in terms of the performance of the technical managing editor, how does it work to be audited based on performance?
Yeah, I mean, we have external channels with clients.
And we were actually just having this conversation today that some of our clients, like, read every single piece of content that goes out.
And some of them have a really good level of trust.
So really, it's about, like, client satisfaction and feedback and content performance.
So it's less about, like, nitpicking the work that you're doing, as long as, like, again, we're meeting contractual obligations.
The client isn't saying, like, we don't like your output.
We need, like, what's going on.
And, like, our content is performing.
Which, you know, the performance part is really, like, up to the account strategy.
Just account director to be like, it's not performing, let's change tactics.
Yeah.
Like this is an execution role really, and like helping execute on strategy.
So like it really does come down to like client satisfaction and trust in like our output.
Okay.
Cool.
Thank you, Andi.
That's really all I had.
All right.
Sheba, it was really great to talk to you.
Thank you, Andi.
was lovely speaking to you as well.
I appreciate the time.
All right.
Have a good day.
You too.
Bye-bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager interview for Technical Managing Editor role at GrowthX with candidate Sheba Anyanwu.](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=240.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX is a services agency using AI-driven workflows to generate content at scale for clients (5+ pieces/week)](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=332.0)
  - [Role combines technical platform management and client-facing project management](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=399.0)
  - [Sheba has extensive experience in content strategy, AI content generation, and technical communication](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=540.0)
  - [Key success factors: ability to communicate technical issues, manage client expectations, and execute on strategy](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX Overview and Role Description

  - [Services agency with internal AI product launched in June](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=337.0)
  - [Generates client content at scale (5+ pieces/week minimum)](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=376.0)
  - [Role combines technical platform management and client-facing project management](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=399.0)
  - [Pod structure: Account Director (strategy), Technical Managing Editor (execution), Content Manager (generation)](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=456.0)
  - [Key responsibilities: communicate platform issues, manage deliverables, edit content, execute strategy](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)

### Candidate Background and Experience

  - [Content strategist with technical communication and editorial expertise](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=540.0)
  - [Worked with startups (Brand Yourself, Story Hunter, Stringer)](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=568.0)
  - [NYU Studio 20 grad program: intersection of editorial and human-computer interaction](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=607.0)
  - [Project management experience at Wall Street Journal and Meta](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=640.0)
  - [Recent role as content engineer at Launch AI for real estate clients](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=697.0)
  - [Managed AI content generation, QA writers, and client expectations](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=754.0)

### AI Content Generation Insights

  - [Designed metrics for AI output and managed expectations](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=808.0)
  - [Created prompt libraries and templatized systems](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=840.0)
  - [Reduced content creation time from 3 days to 8 hours using AI](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=868.0)
  - [Balanced client metrics with internal objectives (e.g., NPS scores)](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=897.0)
  - [Implemented JSON templates for structured data in GPT](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=1103.0)

### GrowthX Content Focus and Metrics

  - [Primary focus on editorial blog posts and SEO audits](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=288.0)
  - [Growing interest in geo-optimization and AI answer optimization](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=1300.0)
  - [Thought leadership content in development](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=1336.0)
  - [Programmatic content generation for location-based services](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=1407.0)
  - [Current metrics: meeting contractual obligations and content performance](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=1653.0)
  - [Future focus on CSAT metrics and more sophisticated performance tracking](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=1230.0)

### Success Factors for Technical Managing Editor

  - [Strong editorial skills](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=540.0)
  - [Ability to communicate technical issues effectively](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=410.0)
  - [Willingness to file detailed bug reports and suggest improvements](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)
  - [Balance between hands-on work and leveraging the platform for scale](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=311.0)

### Performance Evaluation

  - [Based on client satisfaction and feedback](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=1630.0)
  - [Meeting contractual obligations](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=1653.0)
  - [Content performance (monitored by Account Director)](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=1676.0)
  - [Execution of strategy rather than nitpicking individual work](https://fathom.video/share/y7yYmw5H6LSW1BvFq5XLdq7hpHDkvBLP?tab=summary&timestamp=1693.0)

## Next Steps

  - Move Sheba to panel interview stage
  - Sheba to prepare to discuss her process in more detail during panel interview
  - GrowthX to continue developing CSAT metrics and performance tracking

action_items.description: Move Sheba Anyanwu to next stage in hiring process; schedule panel interview
